prose_sections.title: Getting started
prose_sections.markdown:
You're connected — nice. You don't need to learn any commands or tool names.
Just **ask in plain English** and the assistant picks the right MaC tool for you.

**Start with these three:**

1. **See who you are.** Ask: *"Using MaC, who am I and what can I access?"* This
   confirms your sign-in worked and shows your role and the companies you can
   work with.
2. **See what you can do.** Ask: *"What can I do with MaC?"* — you'll get a list
   tailored to your role (the `using-tools` topic).
3. **Pull some real brand context.** Ask: *"What's our brand voice?"* or *"Give
   me an overview of our company."*

**Most common first-time snag:** if you see an empty workspace, you're almost
certainly signed in with a different email than the one your MaC access was
provisioned under. Sign out and back in with your exact provisioned email. See
the `troubleshooting` topic.

When you're ready for the full picture, ask for the `workflows` topic to see
end-to-end examples, or call `get_user_guide` for the complete written guide.

prose_sections.title: What is Marketing as Code?
prose_sections.markdown:
Marketing as Code (MaC) gives your marketing team a single, governed source
for everything that defines your brand — who you are, who you sell to, how you
talk, what you claim, and the proof behind it — and makes all of that available,
on demand, inside the AI tools you already use to write and create.

**The problem it solves.** AI writing tools are only as good as the context you
give them. Without a shared source of truth, every team member pastes in their
own version of the brand voice, the positioning, the audience — and the output
drifts. MaC holds the canonical version of your brand and strategy in one
governed place, so any AI you connect speaks in the same voice and works from
the same facts.

**What you get.** Once connected, you can ask your assistant things like
*"what's our positioning?"* or *"draft this in our brand voice"* and it pulls the
real, approved answer — not a guess. You also get a shared library of
professional marketing frameworks, buyer personas, and document templates to
draw on.

prose_sections.title: How MaC works (the three ideas)
prose_sections.markdown:
You don't need to understand engineering to use MaC — just these three
ideas:

- **The company pack & brand pack.** Everything MaC knows about a company lives
  in a *company pack* — a structured, version-controlled collection of files.
  Inside it, the *brand pack* holds voice, tone, terminology, and visual
  identity; other sections hold positioning and messaging, audiences (ICPs and
  personas), and competitive and market research. Think of it as a living company, brand,
  strategy, and market intelligence book an AI can read instantly and
  natively.
- **The shared marketing library.** Alongside your pack, MaC includes a registry
  of reusable assets: analytical frameworks, buyer/consumer personas, document
  templates, and *writer profiles* that fine-tune the voice of generated
  content. MaC comes with a standard set of writer profiles for the most common
  types of business writing, but you can and should create custom writer
  profiles for your company — just tell your assistant you want to build a
  custom MaC writer profile and it will walk you through it.
- **The MCP server — the bridge.** MaC reaches your AI tools through an MCP
  server (Model Context Protocol) — a secure connector. The AI gains a set of
  *tools* it can call to read your brand and strategy, browse the library, and
  (depending on your access) save work back. You never call these tools by hand
  — you just ask in plain English.

## Your access is tied to your identity

Your **role** on each company determines what you can do. The AI can only ever
see and do what you're permitted to:

| Role | What it means |
|---|---|
| **Viewer** | Read access — view brand, strategy, audiences, and the shared library. |
| **Editor** | Read **and write** — everything a Viewer can do, plus create/update content and save finished work back to the pack. |
| **Owner** | Full access — read, create and save content, and view usage analytics for the company. |
| **Platform Admin** | Full access across companies, plus provisioning and administration. |

**Governance, quietly in the background.** Every piece of content MaC helps
create carries a record of where it came from, and new material can be checked
against your quality rubric. You don't manage any of this — it just keeps your
work consistent and traceable.

prose_sections.title: What you can do (your tools)
prose_sections.markdown:
You don't type tool names — you just ask naturally, and the assistant picks the
right tool. The list below is **personalized to your role**: it shows only what
*you* can do right now, grouped by what you're trying to get done. Each item
includes a plain-English "Try asking" prompt you can use verbatim.

<!-- The role-filtered tool catalog is generated and appended here at call time. -->

prose_sections.title: Example workflows
prose_sections.markdown:
You describe what you want; MaC supplies the context behind the scenes.

- **Draft an on-brand LinkedIn post** — *"Using MaC, draft a LinkedIn post
  announcing our latest release in our brand voice, leading with the value to
  our buyers."* MaC pulls your brand voice and messaging, then drafts in your
  voice, grounded in real positioning and proof points.
- **Build a one-pager** — *"Using MaC, create a one-pager aimed at our primary
  persona, using our positioning, proof points, and the one-pager template."*
  (Editors can then *"save this to our pack."*)
- **Check a draft for alignment** — *"Using MaC, review this draft against our
  brand voice and messaging, and flag anything off-brand or unsupported."*
- **Onboard a new teammate** — *"Using MaC, summarize who we are, who we sell to,
  how we talk, and what we claim."*

## Tips for great results

- **Say "using MaC" at first.** Until you're used to how your assistant behaves,
  naming MaC nudges it to pull your context rather than improvise.
- **Name the audience.** Mentioning a persona ("for platform engineering leads")
  lets MaC tailor tone and substance.
- **Ask for the source.** Want to verify? Ask *"which proof points support
  this?"* — MaC works from real files, so it can show its basis.
- **Save what's good** (Editors). When a draft lands, ask MaC to save it back so
  the brand brain keeps growing.
- **Trust the guardrails.** If MaC declines to invent a statistic or won't claim
  a customer it can't verify, that's the governance working in your favor.

prose_sections.title: Troubleshooting
prose_sections.markdown:
| If you see… | Likely cause | What to do |
|---|---|---|
| An empty workspace / no access | Signed in with the wrong email | Sign out and sign back in with your exact provisioned email. This is the single most common onboarding mistake. |
| A sign-in window that won't complete | Pop-up blocked, or session expired | Allow pop-ups for the client, then retry the connection / re-authenticate. |
| The AI "doesn't see" MaC tools | Connector not enabled for that chat | Enable MaC for the conversation (the + / tools menu), or start a new chat. |
| ChatGPT can't add MaC, or can read but not save | Custom MCP connectors are gated by ChatGPT plan (Developer Mode) | Check OpenAI's current Developer Mode guidance; for write actions, use Claude, Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor. |
| Tools listed but a call fails | Temporary server or auth hiccup | Try once more; if it persists, re-authenticate, then contact your MaC administrator. |

To confirm your connection at any time, ask: *"Using MaC, who am I and what can
I access?"* — the assistant should report your name, your role, and the
companies you can access.

For access changes — adding a teammate, or adjusting roles — contact your MaC
platform administrator, who can provision people in moments.

prose_sections.title: Glossary
prose_sections.markdown:
| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **MaC (Marketing as Code)** | The system that holds your brand and marketing intelligence in one governed place and serves it to your AI tools. |
| **Company pack** | The structured collection of files that holds everything MaC knows about a company. |
| **Brand pack** | The part of the company pack covering voice, tone, terminology, and visual identity. |
| **MCP server** | The secure bridge your AI client connects to in order to reach MaC. |
| **Connector / app** | What an AI client calls a configured link to an MCP server like MaC. |
| **Clerk** | The sign-in service that authenticates you and protects access. |
| **Owner / Editor / Viewer** | Access levels that determine whether you can read, create, and save content. |
| **Persona / ICP** | A profile of a target buyer (ICP = Ideal Customer Profile) used to tailor messaging. |
| **MDR (Marketing Decision Record)** | A durable, written record of a marketing decision and the reasoning behind it. |
| **BYOC** | "Bring your own cloud / repo" — keeping your pack in your own GitHub repository. |
| **Provenance** | The automatic record of where a piece of content came from. |

skills.summary: Creates and updates company packs and brand packs — runs onboarding interviews, crawls company URLs, and ingests brand documents.
Creates, edits, and exports branded marketing documents and media across formats, applying brand voice and messaging from the company pack.
Reviews, audits, and rewrites marketing content against MaC writing quality standards.
A structured B2B and B2C go-to-market strategy skill backed by analytical frameworks and buyer/consumer personas.
Creates and delivers a personalized MaC Getting Started Guide for a specific user.

skills_note: This is a small representative sample (the 5 skills bundled in the MaC platform plugin), not the full governed skills registry — enumerating the complete live registry requires a connected MCP session (list_skills), which this offline snapshot cannot query.
